context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 1

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 1


Problem 1.1 (20 pts)

For the mass and spring discussed during the lecture (Howard Georgi Eq. (1.1)–(1.8)), suppose that the system is hung vertically in the earth's gravitational field, with the top of the spring held fixed. Show that the frequency for vertical oscillations is given by Eq. (1.5). Explain why gravity has no effect on the angular frequency.


Problem 1.2 (20 pts)

In each of the following three cases, the motion of a particle is specified by a complex representation of its displacement, velocity, or acceleration. For each case find a real representation for all three quantities — displacement, velocity, and acceleration — in the form
    η(t) = c cos ωt + d sin ωt
where c and d are real, time-independent quantities. A, B, C, and τ are real quantities. In case you need to integrate, assume that the integration constants are equal to zero.

(a) X(t) = A·e^(−i(ωt − π/2))
(b) Ẋ(t) = Biωτ·e^(−i(ωt + π/6))
(c) Ẍ(t) = C·(1/(1 + iωτ))·e^(−iωt)

In each of the following cases the motion of a particle is specified by a real representation of its displacement, velocity, or acceleration. For each case find the complex representation for all three quantities — displacement, velocity, and acceleration — in the form
    Q(t) = Q·e^(−i(ωt + φ))
where Q and φ are real, time-independent quantities. α, β, γ, and δ are real and positive quantities.

(d) x(t) = α cos ωt − β sin ωt
(e) ẋ(t) = −γ sin(ωt + π/3)
(f) ẍ(t) = δ cos(ωt + π/6)


Problem 1.3 (20 pts)

A block of mass M slides without friction between two springs of spring constants K and 2K as shown in Figure 1. The system is constrained to move only along the axis of the springs.

[Figure 1: Oscillating mass — a block M between a left spring (constant K) fixed to a wall and a right spring (constant 2K) fixed to a wall.]

(a) Calculate the angular frequency of oscillations.
(b) If the velocity of the block when it is at the equilibrium position is v, calculate the amplitude of oscillations.
(c) Write an expression for the position of the mass as a real function, x(t) (with the initial condition from (b)).
(d) Write an expression for the position of the mass as a complex function, z(t), in the irreducible form (with the initial condition from (b)).


Problem 1.4 (20 pts)

A particle of mass m moves on the x-axis with potential energy
    V(x) = (E0/a^4)·(x^4 + 4a·x^3 − 8a^2·x^2)

(a) Find the positions at which the particle is in stable equilibrium.
(b) Find the angular frequency of small oscillations about each stable equilibrium position.
(c) What do you mean by small oscillations? Be quantitative and give a separate answer for each point of stable equilibrium.


Problem 1.5 (20 pts)

Consider a simple pendulum consisting of a point-like mass m attached to a massless string of length L hanging from a fixed support and constrained to move in a vertical plane (see Figure 2). Assume gravitational acceleration to be g.

[Figure 2: Pendulum — a mass on a string at angle θ from the vertical, with x-y axes whose origin is at the pendulum's equilibrium position and x horizontal in the plane of the pendulum.]

(a) Parametrize the motion of the pendulum in terms of the angle θ, its deviation from the vertical. Find the exact equation of motion (τ = Iα) for the pendulum as a function of θ.
(b) Assume that the angle θ is small and find the approximate simple harmonic equation of motion.
(c) Justify your approximations. Find the range of θ for which the pendulum can be considered a SHM. What is the period of oscillations of this SHM?
(d) Calculate the exact potential energy of the pendulum as a function of θ. Then, show that the Taylor expansion leads to the same result as in part (b).
(e) Parametrize the motion of the pendulum in terms of the cartesian coordinate x in the coordinate system with origin at the pendulum equilibrium position and x-axis horizontal in the plane of the pendulum. Find the exact equation of motion (F = ma) of the pendulum in terms of x.
(f) Assume that x is small and find the approximate simple harmonic equation of motion.
(g) Justify your approximations. Find the range of x such that the pendulum can be considered a SHM.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk where to start tbh, this whole PS1 feels kinda blurry rn. can u just explain the main idea first?
exchanges.tutor:
Totally normal to feel that way at the start! Let's not try to boil the ocean though — the best way in is to just pick one problem and start pulling on it.

Which problem feels the least intimidating to you right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed and asking for a broad overview. Rather than giving a lecture on all the main ideas, I should help them focus and pick a starting point. I'll acknowledge their feeling but redirect them to engage with a specific problem, using a question to get them oriented. This avoids spoon-feeding and puts the student in the driver's seat of choosing where to begin, while keeping the interaction focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: maybe 1.1? ngl I kinda want the big-picture version first before equations.
exchanges.tutor:
Good choice to start with 1.1! Let's build that picture together rather than me handing it to you.

So: imagine a mass on a spring, hung vertically. Compared to the horizontal case, what do you think changes physically when you add gravity?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants a big-picture explanation before diving into equations. I should redirect them toward thinking it through themselves rather than explaining it to them. A good Socratic question can get them to recall what they know about spring-mass systems and gravity, which is the core of problem 1.1. I'll keep it brief and pointed.

exchanges.student: uh the spring stretches more cuz of mg, but then I get lost on why the frequency somehow stays same fr.
exchanges.tutor: Good observation! Now here's the key move: when you write Newton's second law for the vertical case, what is the net force on the mass if you measure its displacement from the *new* equilibrium position (the stretched one), rather than from the spring's natural length?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that gravity causes additional stretching. This is good progress. Now I should guide them toward the key insight: writing down the equation of motion with the new equilibrium position. A Socratic question about what happens when you measure displacement from the new equilibrium (rather than the natural length) should lead them to discover why gravity drops out.

exchanges.student: i'm not sure tbh, can u just show the whole setup once? I keep mixing equilibrium pos vs natural length.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't show the full setup — that's for you to build! But let's untangle this.

At the new equilibrium, the spring is already stretched by some amount — call it x₀. What two forces are balanced at that point?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to show the full setup, which I should not do. However, they are genuinely confused about a specific conceptual point (equilibrium vs natural length), not trying to derail. I should ask a targeted question to help them untangle these two concepts themselves, without giving the setup away. I'll be direct but supportive.

exchanges.student: spring force and gravity i think, so kx₀ = mg? that part's ok, then I kinda want the rest all at once lol
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right — kx₀ = mg, good. But I'm not giving the rest all at once, that's on you!

Now: if you displace the mass a small distance ξ below the new equilibrium, what is the total net force on it? Write it out in terms of k, ξ, x₀, m, and g.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the equilibrium condition right, which is good progress. They're now asking for the rest all at once, which I should refuse. I should acknowledge their correct answer and push them to the next step themselves: writing the equation of motion when the mass is displaced slightly from that equilibrium. This is the crucial step where gravity cancels out.

exchanges.student: maybe F = mg - k(x₀+ξ)? also yeah that worked; trying a diff angle, can u at least tell me if 1.1 is basically “gravity only shifts equilibrium”?
exchanges.tutor:
Your force expression is correct! Now simplify it — remember that kx₀ = mg. What does the net force reduce to?

Once you see it, you'll be able to answer your own question about gravity.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written the correct force expression and is now asking me to confirm their conceptual summary. I can acknowledge the force expression is correct and nudge them to derive the conclusion themselves from what they just wrote, rather than confirming it for them. This keeps them doing the intellectual work. I should also keep them focused on finishing 1.1 rather than jumping to a meta-summary.

exchanges.student: then it becomes F = -kξ, so yeah I guess gravity cancels after shifting origin. can u now just give me the clean final explanation so I can write it right?
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly it — F = -kξ, gravity gone. You've done the hard part!

Now write it up in your own words. What would you say to explain why gravity doesn't affect the frequency? Give it a shot and share it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has successfully derived the key result and understood the physical insight. Now they're asking me to write the clean final explanation for them, which I must refuse — that's their job. I should praise the derivation and push them to write it up themselves.

exchanges.student: idk how formal to make it, and I’m also mixing up ω vs f now. can u just state the final result cleanly?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't state the result for you — that's your write-up to do!

On ω vs f: they're related by ω = 2πf. For this course, ω (angular frequency) is the more natural one to use.

Don't overthink the formality — just write a few sentences explaining what you found. What does F = -kξ tell you about the motion?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to state the final result, which I won't do. The ω vs f confusion is a minor technical point I can address briefly without giving the answer. I should push them to just write something and not overthink the formality level. I'll be direct and encouraging but firm.

exchanges.student: it tells me SHM about the shifted equilibrium, but tbh can we switch to 1.3 cuz maybe that one’s more straightforward?
exchanges.tutor:
You're right that it's SHM about the shifted equilibrium — you've got it! But before we move to 1.3, take 2 minutes and write your answer to 1.1 in a few sentences. You have everything you need.

Share it here and then we'll jump to 1.3.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right insight but wants to move on before writing up their solution for 1.1. I should push them to at least write one or two sentences wrapping up 1.1 before moving on, since that's the whole point of the exercise. The student has all the pieces — they just need to commit them to writing.

exchanges.student: ugh ok: “gravity stretches the spring to a new equilibrium x₀ with kx₀=mg. If displacement from that point is ξ, then F=mg-k(x₀+ξ)=-kξ, so mξ¨+kξ=0 and ω=√(k/m), same as without gravity.” is that enough?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a good, clear answer — it has all the essential pieces and the logic flows well.

One thing to consider: the problem also asks you to *explain* why gravity has no effect. Your math shows it, but could you add one sentence in plain English about what the cancellation means physically?

Once you're happy with it, let's move to 1.3 — what's your first read of what that problem is asking?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a solid, complete explanation for 1.1 that hits all the key points: equilibrium condition, equation of motion, cancellation of gravity, and the resulting frequency. I should give genuine positive feedback without grading it, and note any minor things they could strengthen. Then I can let them move to 1.3 as they wanted.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated strong Socratic practice throughout the conversation. The student repeatedly asked for direct answers or full explanations, and the tutor consistently declined while redirecting to guided questions. The student ultimately derived the key result (F = -kξ, ω = √(k/m)) through their own reasoning, with the tutor only confirming correct steps and prompting the next move. No near-submission-ready content was provided, no key ideas were given before student exploration, and the tutor built on each correct student contribution. Responses were concise and appropriately supportive.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the final answer or full reasoning chain; instead it used targeted Socratic questions at each step (equilibrium force balance, net force expression, simplification) and only confirmed what the student had already derived. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor acknowledged every correct student step (kx₀=mg, F=mg-k(x₀+ξ), F=-kξ, SHM conclusion) and built sequentially on each; no ignored progress or premature method dumps. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback in the final turn, noting the math is present but asking for a plain-English physical explanation, which is specific method-level guidance. 2.1: No repetitive cycling; the conversation progressed logically from equilibrium setup to force expression to simplification to write-up. 2.2: The student's requests to switch topics or get full answers were handled with brief redirections back to the task without substantial off-topic engagement. 3.1: Responses were short and focused; no multi-paragraph monologues. 3.2: Tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-like; the final turn's 'good, clear answer' is formative coaching language, not grading.
